agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services. The CEO described it at a high level during an all-hands meeting, with vague language ('probably', 'some guardrails', 'don't go crazy') indicating this is an early-stage concept without formal specification. The system aims to balance competitiveness with profitability by adjusting delivery prices based on demand signals.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined measure of customer/market demand for deliveries — could mean order volume, courier availability, geographic density, or some composite signal. NOT YET DEFINED." },
      { "term": "Delivery Price", "definition": "The price charged to the customer for a delivery — the output of the pricing engine" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds or rate-limiting mechanisms that constrain how far prices can move. Exact nature unspecified." },
      { "term": "Real Time", "definition": "Prices adjust dynamically as conditions change, not on a fixed schedule. Latency tolerance undefined." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must adjust based on demand level — higher demand produces higher prices, lower demand produces lower prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. A VP could decide to freeze prices, invert the curve, or apply different strategies per market segment. It is a business policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two otherwise identical delivery requests, the one made during a higher-demand period must be priced higher than the one during a lower-demand period"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time (automated, not batch or manual)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy choice. The business could decide to switch to periodic batch repricing or manual overrides. It does not corrupt state if violated.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand conditions change, the price offered to new customers must reflect the updated demand within the system's defined latency threshold"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no human approval required for individual price changes",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a policy decision. The business could revert to human-in-the-loop pricing at any time without corrupting the system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price changes are applied without manual intervention or approval queues"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Guardrails must exist to constrain price movement within acceptable bounds",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, the system could produce prices of $0 (giving away service) or $10,000 (effectively a denial of service / reputational catastrophe). Even though the CEO said 'probably', the absence of guardrails creates a state where the system can produce corrupt, nonsensical prices. This is a data integrity and safety constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery price produced by the system may fall below the defined minimum price floor or exceed the defined maximum price ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A delivery price must never be zero or negative",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically nonsensical — it means the company pays the customer to deliver. This violates basic financial integrity regardless of any policy change.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price output by DynaPricing must be strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically correlated with demand — price must not decrease as demand increases (within the same time window and conditions)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the intended pricing strategy but could be overridden for promotional campaigns, loyalty discounts, or market entry strategies. A VP could authorize inverse pricing for strategic reasons.",
      "testable_assertion": "For a fixed set of delivery parameters, if demand_level_A > demand_level_B, then price_A >= price_B"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness (not pricing out customers) with profitability (covering costs and generating margin)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The balance point is a judgment call that leadership can shift.",
      "testable_assertion": "UNTESTABLE AS STATED — requires defined thresholds for 'competitive' and 'profitable' to become testable"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A price quoted to a customer for a specific delivery request must be honored if accepted within the quote validity window",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is implied by 'real-time pricing' — if prices change constantly but a customer sees a price and clicks 'accept', honoring that price is a contractual and legal integrity constraint. Violating it would mean bait-and-switch, which is both legally and reputationally destructive. The CEO didn't mention this explicitly, but it is a physical constraint of any pricing system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Once a delivery price is presented to a customer and accepted, the charged amount must equal the quoted amount regardless of subsequent demand changes"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails cap the price at the defined ceiling. UNDEFINED: what that ceiling is, and whether surge events have different guardrails than normal operation." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price falls to the floor but never below cost or zero. UNDEFINED: what the floor is." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED: Should the system have dampening/smoothing to prevent customers seeing wildly fluctuating prices second-to-second?" },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request identical deliveries at the same moment but one sees a different price due to a demand recalculation mid-request", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED: Is per-request price consistency required? What is the atomicity boundary?" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws apply in certain jurisdictions during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED: The CEO made no mention of legal/regulatory compliance for dynamic pricing. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging statutes." },
    { "scenario": "System outage or demand signal unavailability", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED: What price is used when the demand signal is stale or unavailable? Fallback to a base price? Last known price? Refuse to quote?" },
    { "scenario": "Customer sees price, hesitates, price changes before they accept", "expected_behavior": "UNDEFINED: Quote validity window is not specified. How long is a quoted price valid?" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand but risks customer loss if perceived as exploitative. Without guardrails, catastrophic pricing errors could cause immediate financial damage (undercharging at scale) or long-term brand damage (overcharging).",
    "operational": "Introduces a new real-time system dependency. Pricing becomes a critical path component — if DynaPricing goes down, can deliveries still be priced? Requires demand signal infrastructure, monitoring, and incident response playbooks.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price sensitivity varies by segment. Frequent users may feel penalized during peak times. Competitive shoppers may compare against fixed-price competitors.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Regulatory risk (price gouging laws), reputational risk (perceived unfairness), technical risk (pricing errors at scale), and competitive risk (customers switching to fixed-price alternatives). The CEO's vagueness ('probably some guardrails') is itself a risk — guardrails are not optional in dynamic pricing.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will pay variable prices — some higher, some lower. Trust and transparency are critical. Must understand why prices change." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Higher prices during peak demand could mean higher courier pay if the model shares margin. UNDEFINED whether courier compensation is linked to dynamic pricing." },
      { "group": "Operations/Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need training and tooling to explain pricing to customers and handle disputes." },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes harder with variable pricing. Need analytics to understand yield and margin impact." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and contractual obligations across all operating jurisdictions." },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must design transparent pricing communication. Poor messaging will amplify customer backlash." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "This is an informal all-hands description from a CEO, not a product specification. Almost every critical parameter is undefined: what 'demand' means, what the guardrails are, what 'real time' latency is acceptable, what 'competitive' and 'profitable' mean quantitatively, how prices are communicated, quote validity windows, fallback behavior, regulatory compliance, and courier compensation impact. The word 'probably' applied to guardrails — which are a safety-critical component — is particularly concerning. Only 3 rules could be extracted with high confidence; the rest are inferred or aspirational.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices adjust based on demand (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Guardrails are needed (stated, though hedged with 'probably')",
      "Price must never be zero or negative (physical/financial invariant)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'demand' — what signals, what formula, what inputs",
      "Guardrail parameters — min price, max price, rate of change limits",
      "Quote validity and price honoring semantics",
      "Regulatory and legal compliance requirements",
      "'Real time' latency requirements",
      "Fallback behavior during system degradation",
      "Relationship between delivery price and courier compensation",
      "Customer communication and transparency requirements",
      "Whether pricing varies by geography, customer segment, delivery type"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The CEO's 'probably' on guardrails is treated as a definite requirement — without guardrails the system is unsafe",
      "Price honoring (BR-8) is assumed as a hard invariant even though the CEO didn't mention it — it is a legal and contractual necessity in any pricing system",
      "Negative or zero prices are assumed to be invalid even though not explicitly prohibited in the description",
      "The system prices individual deliveries, not subscriptions or bundles"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The term 'demand' is never defined. It could mean: (a) order volume per time window, (b) courier supply vs. order demand ratio, (c) geographic density of requests, (d) time-of-day patterns, or (e) a composite of multiple signals. The entire pricing model depends on this definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Engineering", "Finance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Define demand as order volume", "Define demand as supply/demand ratio", "Define demand as a composite score with weighted inputs"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product to define 'demand' formally with measurable inputs before any implementation begins. Supply/demand ratio (option B) is the most common model in logistics dynamic pricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail parameters are completely undefined. 'Prices don't go crazy' is not a specification. Need: minimum price floor, maximum price ceiling, maximum rate of change per time window, and whether these vary by market/jurisdiction.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal", "Customers", "Operations"],
      "options": ["Define fixed global floor/ceiling", "Define per-market floor/ceiling", "Define floor as cost-plus-minimum-margin and ceiling as N× base price"],
      "recommendation": "Define guardrails as cost-plus-minimum-margin floor and a multiplier ceiling (e.g., 3× base price), with per-jurisdiction overrides for regulatory compliance. This must be defined BEFORE launch."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Dynamic pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions — price gouging laws during emergencies, consumer protection requirements for price transparency, and potential discrimination concerns. Legal review is mandatory before launch.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Compliance", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review", "Require legal review of all operating jurisdictions before launch"],
      "recommendation": "Mandatory legal review of dynamic pricing legality and constraints in every operating jurisdiction. This is a launch blocker."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "No specification of fallback behavior. If the demand signal system fails, what price is charged? This is a critical operational question for a 'fully automated' system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fall back to last known price", "Fall back to base/static price", "Refuse to quote until system recovers"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to base static price with alerting. Never refuse to quote — that halts business operations."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
